walkthrough.title: Open the Box Carefully
walkthrough.text: Welcome to Pandemic Legacy Season One. Before we do anything, let's talk about what makes this game special. This is a legacy game, which means everything we do is permanent. We will put stickers on the board, write on cards, and even destroy cards by tearing them up. None of that happens yet, but keep it in mind as we set up. Let's open the box and take a look at what is inside.

walkthrough.title: Identify the Components
walkthrough.text: Inside the box we will find the game board, which is a world map showing cities connected by lines. We also have a deck of player cards, a deck of infection cards, disease cubes in four colors, six research station pieces, and a set of character cards. There are also sealed dossiers and numbered boxes. Do not open any of these sealed components yet. We will only open them when the game tells us to.

walkthrough.title: Find the Legacy Deck
walkthrough.text: Now let's locate the Legacy deck. This is a special deck of cards that drives the story forward as we play through the campaign. Set it face down near the board without looking through it. We will draw from this deck when specific events happen during the game. The Legacy deck is the heart of what makes this game different from regular Pandemic.

walkthrough.title: Unfold the Game Board
walkthrough.text: Let's unfold the game board and place it in the center of the table. We will see a map of the world with 48 cities connected by red lines. The cities are color-coded into four regions: blue cities in North America and Europe, yellow cities in South America and Africa, black cities in the Middle East and South Asia, and red cities in East Asia and the Pacific. Along the edges we will find the outbreak track and the infection rate track.

walkthrough.title: Set the Outbreak Track
walkthrough.text: Find the outbreak marker and place it on the zero space of the outbreak track. This track goes from zero to eight. If the outbreak marker ever reaches eight, we immediately lose the game. Every time a city outbreaks, this marker moves up one space. Keeping this under control is one of our most important jobs as a team.

walkthrough.title: Set the Infection Rate
walkthrough.text: Now find the infection rate marker and place it on the first space of the infection rate track. This track shows how many infection cards we draw at the end of each turn. It starts at two cards per turn and increases as epidemics happen. The more epidemics we trigger, the faster diseases spread across the board.

walkthrough.title: Place the Cure Markers
walkthrough.text: Find the four cure markers, which are small vials. Place them vial-side up near the discovered cures area of the board. When we discover a cure for a disease, we will flip the matching marker to show it has been cured. Curing all four diseases before time runs out is our primary goal in the prologue.

walkthrough.title: Sort the Disease Cubes
walkthrough.text: Let's sort the disease cubes by color. We have blue, yellow, black, and red cubes, with 24 of each color. Place them in four separate piles near the board where everyone can reach them. These cubes represent the spread of disease across the world. If we ever need to place a cube and there are none left in that color's supply, we lose the game immediately.

walkthrough.title: Place Atlanta Research Station
walkthrough.text: Find the research station pieces. These are small white structures. Place one research station in Atlanta on the game board. Atlanta is our starting headquarters and the home of the CDC. We have five remaining research stations that can be built during the game. Research stations are critical because we need to be at one to discover a cure.

walkthrough.title: Seed the Initial Infections
walkthrough.text: Now let's infect the world. Shuffle the infection deck and draw three cards. Place three disease cubes of the matching color on each of those three cities. Draw three more cards and place two cubes on each. Draw three final cards and place one cube on each. That is nine cities infected: three with three cubes, three with two cubes, and three with one cube. Place all nine drawn infection cards face up in the infection discard pile.

walkthrough.title: Choose Characters
walkthrough.text: Each player now picks a character to play. Look through the available role cards. Each character has a unique special ability printed on their card. Choose a character whose ability appeals to you. In this campaign, your character will persist across every session, gaining upgrades and potentially scars over time. Pick someone you want to stick with for the long haul.

walkthrough.title: Place Pawns and Draw Starting Hands
walkthrough.text: Place your character's pawn in Atlanta. Everyone starts at the CDC headquarters. Now let's prepare the player deck. Remove the epidemic cards for now, then shuffle the remaining player cards. Deal starting hands based on player count: four cards each for two players, three cards each for three players, or two cards each for four players. Keep your cards visible to all players since this is a cooperative game and open discussion is encouraged.

walkthrough.title: Add Epidemic Cards to the Player Deck
walkthrough.text: Now we need to add the epidemic cards back into the player deck. For your first game, we recommend using four epidemic cards for a standard difficulty. Divide the remaining player deck into four roughly equal face-down piles. Shuffle one epidemic card into each pile without looking at the other cards. Then stack the four piles on top of each other to form the final player deck. This ensures epidemics are spread throughout the deck rather than clumped together.

walkthrough.title: Ready to Play
walkthrough.text: We are all set up and ready to save the world. Let's pick a first player. The rulebook suggests the player who was most recently sick goes first, but any method works. Remember, this is a cooperative game. We win or lose together. On our turns we will take actions, draw cards, and deal with infections. Let's learn how that works.

Do NOT open sealed dossiers or numbered boxes yet

Turn structure: actions → draw cards → infect cities

walkthrough.title: Turn Overview
walkthrough.text: Each turn in Pandemic follows three steps in order. First, we take up to four actions. Second, we draw two player cards. Third, we infect cities by drawing infection cards. After that, play passes to the next player clockwise. Let's walk through each of these steps in detail.

walkthrough.title: Actions: Drive or Ferry
walkthrough.text: Our first action option is Drive or Ferry. This lets us move our pawn from one city to an adjacent connected city. Look at the red lines on the board connecting cities. We can move along any of those lines for one action. This is our most basic way to get around, but it is slow for crossing the globe.

walkthrough.title: Actions: Direct Flight
walkthrough.text: To travel faster, we can use Direct Flight. Discard a city card from our hand to move our pawn directly to that city, no matter where we are on the board. This is like buying a plane ticket. It costs a card, but it lets us jump across the world in one action. This is one of our most powerful movement options.

walkthrough.title: Actions: Charter Flight
walkthrough.text: Charter Flight works the other way around. If we have the card matching the city we are currently in, we can discard it to fly to any city on the board. Think of it as chartering a private jet from our current location. It costs one action and one specific card, but it takes us literally anywhere.

walkthrough.title: Actions: Shuttle Flight
walkthrough.text: Shuttle Flight lets us move between any two cities that both have research stations. If we are standing in a city with a research station and there is another research station somewhere on the board, we can hop between them for one action without discarding any cards. This is why building research stations in strategic locations is so valuable.

walkthrough.title: Actions: Build a Research Station
walkthrough.text: To build a research station, we discard the city card matching the city we are currently in. Place one of the research station pieces on that city. We can only have six stations on the board at once, so if we need a seventh, we must move one from somewhere else. Stations are essential for discovering cures and for shuttle travel between them.

walkthrough.title: Actions: Treat Disease
walkthrough.text: The Treat Disease action removes one disease cube from the city we are in. Just pick up one cube of any color present and return it to the supply. If we have already discovered the cure for that color, treating is even better. We remove all cubes of that cured color from the city with a single action instead of just one.

walkthrough.title: Actions: Share Knowledge
walkthrough.text: Share Knowledge lets us give or take a city card with another player, but only if both players are in the city that matches the card. For example, if two of us are standing in Paris, one of us can give the Paris card to the other. This must happen in the named city. Coordinating card sharing is crucial for collecting the five cards needed to discover a cure.

walkthrough.title: Actions: Discover a Cure
walkthrough.text: This is our main objective. To discover a cure, we must be at a research station and discard five city cards of the same color. When we do, flip the matching cure marker to show that disease is now cured. Curing a disease does not remove cubes from the board. It just means that treating that disease is now more efficient and we are one step closer to winning. We win as soon as all four cures are discovered.

walkthrough.title: Drawing Player Cards
walkthrough.text: After taking our four actions, we draw two cards from the player deck. Most cards are city cards that we add to our hand. But sometimes we draw an epidemic card. That is bad news, and we will cover what happens next. Our hand limit is seven cards. If we ever have more than seven at the end of the draw step, we must discard down to seven immediately.

walkthrough.title: Epidemic Cards
walkthrough.text: When we draw an epidemic card, three things happen in order. First, increase the infection rate by moving the marker one space to the right. Second, infect a new city by drawing the bottom card of the infection deck and placing three cubes of that color on that city. Third, intensify by shuffling the infection discard pile and placing it on top of the remaining infection deck. This is the most dangerous mechanic in the game because cities that were already infected will get infected again.

walkthrough.title: The Infect Cities Step
walkthrough.text: The last thing we do on our turn is draw infection cards equal to the current infection rate and place one matching cube on each drawn city. If a city already has three cubes of that color and we need to add another, that city does not get a fourth cube. Instead, it outbreaks. Place the drawn infection cards in the discard pile after resolving them.

walkthrough.title: Outbreaks
walkthrough.text: When a city outbreaks, move the outbreak marker up one space on the track. Then place one cube of the outbreak color on every city connected to the outbreak city. If any of those neighboring cities also already had three cubes and just received a fourth, they outbreak too, creating a chain reaction. A single outbreak can cascade across the entire board if we have let cubes build up.

walkthrough.title: Chain Outbreaks
walkthrough.text: Chain outbreaks are the deadliest threat in the game. When an outbreak causes a neighboring city to also outbreak, that is a chain. Each city can only outbreak once per chain reaction, so we will never loop back to the same city. But the outbreak marker still moves up for each city that outbreaks. Two or three chain outbreaks can push us dangerously close to losing.

walkthrough.title: Event Cards
walkthrough.text: Mixed in with the player deck are event cards. These are special one-time-use cards that give us powerful advantages. We can play an event card at almost any time, even on another player's turn, and it does not cost an action. Events do not count toward our hand limit of seven. Use them wisely because once played, they are gone for good.

walkthrough.title: Character Abilities
walkthrough.text: Each character has a unique ability that bends the rules in some way. For example, the Medic removes all cubes of one color when treating instead of just one, even without a cure. The Researcher can give any city card to another player in the same city, not just the matching one. Read your character's ability carefully and talk with the team about how to use it best. These abilities are what make each player's role feel different.

walkthrough.title: Eradication
walkthrough.text: If we have discovered the cure for a disease and we manage to remove every single cube of that color from the board, the disease is eradicated. Flip the cure marker to the sunset side. An eradicated disease no longer has any effect. When we draw infection cards for that color, we simply ignore them. Eradication is powerful but not required to win.

walkthrough.title: Three Ways to Lose
walkthrough.text: We lose the game immediately if any of three things happen. First, the outbreak marker reaches eight. Second, we need to place a disease cube but there are none left in that color's supply. Third, we need to draw a player card but the deck is empty. The player deck running out is essentially a timer. We have a limited number of turns to discover all four cures before time runs out.

walkthrough.title: Winning the Game
walkthrough.text: We win as soon as we discover the fourth and final cure. We do not need to remove all cubes from the board. We do not need to eradicate anything. The instant that fourth cure is discovered, we win, even if the board is covered in cubes. Focus on collecting sets of five same-colored cards and getting them to a research station.

walkthrough.title: Legacy: Permanent Consequences
walkthrough.text: Here is where Pandemic Legacy diverges from regular Pandemic. After each game, win or lose, we apply permanent changes to the board and our characters. Cities that outbreak may receive damage stickers that make them harder to deal with in future games. Characters who are in a city during an outbreak may receive scars that impose lasting penalties. These changes carry forward through the entire campaign.

walkthrough.title: Legacy: The Campaign Structure
walkthrough.text: The campaign spans twelve in-game months, from January to December. Each month presents new objectives and story developments. If we lose a month, we get one chance to replay it. Win or lose the replay, we move on to the next month. This means the campaign lasts between twelve and twenty-four sessions total depending on how often we need replays.

walkthrough.title: Legacy: Funding Level
walkthrough.text: After each game, our funding level adjusts. If we won, funding decreases slightly, meaning fewer helpful event cards next game. If we lost, funding increases, giving us more event cards and making the next game a bit easier. This is the campaign's built-in catch-up mechanism that keeps things balanced no matter how well or poorly we are doing.

walkthrough.title: Legacy: Character Upgrades
walkthrough.text: Between games, we can apply upgrade stickers to our characters. These upgrades improve abilities and can be earned through gameplay or story events. We can also establish relationships between characters who end a game in the same city, granting them a bonus when they work together. Choose upgrades carefully because they are permanent.

walkthrough.title: Legacy: Objectives
walkthrough.text: Each month has mandatory objectives that we must complete to win. In the prologue and early months, the mandatory objective is simply to discover all four cures. As the campaign progresses, new objectives will be revealed through the Legacy deck. Some months also have optional objectives that award bonuses. Always check the objective card at the start of each session.

walkthrough.title: Legacy: No Spoilers
walkthrough.text: The story of Pandemic Legacy unfolds through the Legacy deck and sealed components. We will not reveal any specifics here. All we will say is this: the game will surprise us. Rules will change. New mechanics will appear. The story will take unexpected turns. Trust the process, follow the instructions when they appear, and enjoy the ride. Everything we have taught so far covers the base rules needed for the prologue and January.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Practice Session
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first practice round of Pandemic Legacy. We will walk through a few turns of a scripted four-player game so everyone can see how the turn structure works in practice. Even if you have fewer players, follow along to learn the flow. Our four characters start in Atlanta with a research station already there. Let's save the world.

walkthrough.title: Check the Board State
walkthrough.text: Before anyone takes a turn, let's look at the board. We seeded nine cities during setup. Find the three cities with three cubes on them. These are our hot spots and need immediate attention. If any of them get infected again before we treat them, they will outbreak. Our first priority as a team is deciding who goes where to keep those hot spots under control.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: Moving Toward a Hot Spot
walkthrough.text: Player 1, let's check your hand. You should see city cards of various colors. Find a card that matches one of the cities with three cubes on it, or a city near one. Use your first action to Drive or Ferry toward the nearest three-cube city. If you have a card for a hot spot city, use Direct Flight to get there in one action instead. Remember, you have four actions total this turn.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: Treating Disease
walkthrough.text: Now that Player 1 is at or near a three-cube city, spend actions to Treat Disease. Each Treat Disease action removes one cube. If you made it to the hot spot in one action, you can treat three times with your remaining three actions, clearing that city completely. That is a great first turn. Keeping three-cube cities clear prevents outbreaks.

walkthrough.title: Player 1: Draw and Infect
walkthrough.text: Player 1 is done with actions. Draw two cards from the player deck. Check if either card is an epidemic. If not, just add the city cards to your hand. Now draw infection cards equal to the current infection rate, which should be two. Place one cube on each drawn city. Watch to see if either city already has three cubes. If not, place the cubes and move on.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake: Not Communicating
walkthrough.text: Here is a mistake we see all the time in first games. Players take their turns in silence, making decisions alone. This is a fully cooperative game. Before each turn, talk with the team. Where are the danger zones? Who has cards in which colors? Who is closest to a hot spot? Five seconds of discussion before each turn prevents wasted actions and avoids situations where two players treat the same city while another one outbreaks.

walkthrough.title: Player 2: Building a Research Station
walkthrough.text: Player 2, look at your hand. Do you have the card for a city that would make a good research station location? We already have one in Atlanta, but we want stations spread across the map for shuttle travel and curing. If you have a card for a central hub city, spend one action to Drive or Fly there and another action to build a research station by discarding that city's card.

walkthrough.title: Why Station Placement Matters
walkthrough.text: Research stations serve two purposes. We need them to discover cures, and they allow free Shuttle Flight movement between any two stations. A station in Europe and one in Asia means we can hop between continents in a single action with no card cost. Think about station placement as building our team's transportation network. Two or three well-placed stations can save us dozens of actions over the course of the game.

walkthrough.title: Player 2: Draw and Infect
walkthrough.text: Player 2 draws two player cards and then draws infection cards. Let's say one of the infection cards matches a city that already has two cubes. That city now has three cubes, making it a new hot spot. Flag this for the team. Someone needs to get there before the next infection hits that city, or it will outbreak.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake: Hoarding Cards
walkthrough.text: A common mistake in early games is holding onto cards that you will never use for a cure. If you have five blue cards spread across three players, you need to start planning Share Knowledge actions to consolidate them. But if you are holding onto a random yellow card just in case, consider whether that card slot is better used elsewhere. Remember, your hand limit is seven. Getting stuck at the limit and being forced to discard a useful card is a painful mistake we can avoid by planning ahead.

walkthrough.title: Player 3: Working Toward a Cure
walkthrough.text: Player 3, look at your hand for same-colored city cards. How many do you have of one color? If you have three or four of one color, you are close to discovering a cure. The whole team should start thinking about how to get you the remaining cards. Use your actions this turn to move toward a research station and into position for a Share Knowledge exchange in future turns.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake: Forgetting Share Knowledge Rules
walkthrough.text: Here is a rules mistake that trips up many new players. Share Knowledge requires both players to be in the city that matches the card being shared. You cannot hand off a Paris card while standing in London. Both players must physically be in Paris. This means coordinating movement across multiple turns. Plan these exchanges two or three turns ahead so you are not wasting actions.

walkthrough.title: Player 3: Draw and Infect
walkthrough.text: Player 3 draws two player cards. Let's say one of them is an epidemic card. Do not panic. Follow the three epidemic steps carefully. First, move the infection rate marker right. Second, draw the bottom card of the infection deck and place three cubes on that city. Third, shuffle the infection discard pile and put it on top of the infection deck. Take your time with this. Rushing through epidemics is how mistakes happen.

walkthrough.title: Understanding the Intensify Step
walkthrough.text: The intensify step is the most important part of an epidemic and the key to understanding why Pandemic is so tense. By shuffling the discard pile onto the top of the infection deck, cities that were already infected will be drawn again soon. That three-cube city we just treated? Its card is back on top of the deck. This is why treating hot spots before epidemics is critical. After an epidemic, reassess the board and plan your next moves accordingly.

walkthrough.title: Player 4: Fire Fighting
walkthrough.text: Player 4, the epidemic just made things worse. Check the board for cities with three cubes. You are the firefighter this turn. Spend your actions getting to the most dangerous city and treating it. Use Direct Flight if you have the right card. Every cube you remove from a three-cube city is insurance against an outbreak on the next infection draw.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake: Ignoring the Cube Supply
walkthrough.text: Keep an eye on the disease cube supply piles. If one color is getting low, that means there are a lot of cubes on the board. If that supply runs out while we need to place another cube of that color, we lose immediately. When a cube supply drops below five, treat it as a red alert. Someone needs to start treating that color aggressively. Running out of cubes is one of the sneakiest ways to lose because players forget to watch the supply.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake: Splitting Up Too Much
walkthrough.text: Another beginner mistake is sending each player to a different corner of the map. While it seems efficient, it makes Share Knowledge impossible because players are never in the same city. At least two players should be working in the same region at any given time. Balance fire fighting with cure progress. One or two players should focus on treating while the others collect and share cards for cures.

walkthrough.title: Planning the First Cure
walkthrough.text: After a few rounds, the team should be close to a first cure. Look at which color has the most cards spread across all players. Count them up. We need five same-colored cards on one player at a research station. That means coordinating Share Knowledge actions and movement. Map out the steps: who has the cards, where will they meet, how many turns will it take? Plan this as a team.

walkthrough.title: Discovering the First Cure
walkthrough.text: The moment of triumph. When one player has five same-colored city cards and is standing at a research station, they spend one action to Discover a Cure. Discard all five cards and flip the cure marker. Now when anyone treats that disease, they remove all cubes of that color from the city instead of just one. This is a huge power boost. Three more cures to go.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake: Forgetting Legacy Triggers
walkthrough.text: In Pandemic Legacy, certain events trigger Legacy deck draws. The game will tell us when to draw from the Legacy deck, but it is easy to miss triggers in the heat of gameplay. Assign one player as the Legacy deck watcher. Their job is to remind the team when a trigger condition is met. Missing a Legacy trigger can cause confusion later in the campaign.

walkthrough.title: The Race Against Time
walkthrough.text: As the player deck runs down, we are racing against the clock. Count the remaining cards in the player deck. Divide by two to estimate how many turns we have left. If we need eight more turns to finish but only have twelve cards in the deck, that is six turns. We need to prioritize cures over treating. Sometimes the board has to get messy so we can focus on winning before time runs out.

walkthrough.title: End of Game: Win or Lose
walkthrough.text: Whether we win by curing all four diseases or lose to outbreaks, cubes, or the deck running out, the legacy part begins. Apply any permanent changes the game tells us to. Check for city damage stickers, character scars, and end-of-game instructions. Record the result. If we lost, we get one more chance at this month before moving on.

walkthrough.title: Campaign Tips
walkthrough.text: Here are three things to keep in mind going forward. First, protect your characters. Scars accumulate over the campaign, and a heavily scarred character becomes a liability. Second, protect key cities. Once a city starts accumulating damage stickers, it gets harder every game. Third, enjoy the story. The Legacy deck will take the game in directions you do not expect. Let yourself be surprised.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations on completing your first practice session of Pandemic Legacy. You have seen how to move around the board, treat diseases, handle epidemics, share knowledge, and work toward cures. You have also learned about the most common mistakes new players make: not communicating, hoarding cards, forgetting Share Knowledge rules, ignoring cube supplies, and splitting up too much. You are ready for the real campaign.

walkthrough.title: Before You Begin the Campaign
walkthrough.text:
Before you start your first real game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics on treating, curing, and managing the board.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to all actions, terms, and card types.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Good luck saving the world!

summary.title: Welcome to the Practice Session
bullets: Scripted tutorial with 4 players demonstrating core mechanics
Follow along even with fewer players
All characters start in Atlanta with a research station

summary.title: Check the Board State
bullets: Identify 3 cities with 3 cubes — these are hot spots
Any reinfection on these cities = outbreak
Team priority: decide who addresses which hot spot

summary.title: Player 1: Moving Toward a Hot Spot
bullets: Check hand for cards matching 3-cube cities
Drive/Ferry or Direct Flight to reach a hot spot
4 actions total per turn

summary.title: Player 1: Treating Disease
bullets: Each Treat Disease action removes 1 cube
Reach hot spot in 1 action → treat 3 times to clear city
Prevents outbreaks on future infections

summary.title: Player 1: Draw and Infect
bullets: Draw 2 player cards (check for epidemics)
Draw infection cards = infection rate (starts at 2)
Place 1 cube per city drawn

summary.title: Common Mistake: Not Communicating
bullets: Pandemic is fully cooperative — discuss every turn
Coordinate who goes where before acting
5 seconds of planning prevents wasted actions

summary.title: Player 2: Building a Research Station
bullets: Discard matching city card → build station
Stations needed for cures and shuttle travel
Spread stations across the map for coverage

summary.title: Why Station Placement Matters
bullets: Stations enable Shuttle Flight (free movement between stations)
2-3 well-placed stations save dozens of actions
Think of stations as your transport network

summary.title: Player 2: Draw and Infect
bullets: New 3-cube city created by infection
Flag new hot spots to the team immediately
Someone must treat before next infection hits

summary.title: Common Mistake: Hoarding Cards
bullets: Hand limit = 7; forced discard is painful
Plan Share Knowledge to consolidate colors for cures
Don't hold cards you'll never use for a cure

summary.title: Player 3: Working Toward a Cure
bullets: Count same-color cards across all players
3-4 of one color = close to a cure
Start positioning for Share Knowledge exchanges

summary.title: Common Mistake: Forgetting Share Knowledge Rules
bullets: Both players must be in the city matching the card
Can't hand off Paris card while standing in London
Plan exchanges 2-3 turns ahead

summary.title: Player 3: Draw and Infect
bullets: Epidemic drawn — follow 3 steps carefully
Increase → Infect (bottom card, 3 cubes) → Intensify (shuffle discard on top)
Don't rush; take time to resolve correctly

summary.title: Understanding the Intensify Step
bullets: Discard pile shuffled onto TOP of infection deck
Previously infected cities will be drawn again soon
Treat hot spots BEFORE epidemics when possible

summary.title: Player 4: Fire Fighting
bullets: Post-epidemic: check for new 3-cube cities
Use Direct Flight to reach most dangerous city
Every cube removed from 3-cube city = outbreak insurance

summary.title: Common Mistake: Ignoring the Cube Supply
bullets: Running out of any cube color = instant loss
Below 5 cubes remaining = red alert
Treat that color aggressively when supply is low

summary.title: Common Mistake: Splitting Up Too Much
bullets: All 4 players in separate corners = can't Share Knowledge
Keep 2+ players in same region for card exchanges
Balance treating with cure progress

summary.title: Planning the First Cure
bullets: Count cards by color across all players
Need 5 same-color on 1 player at a research station
Map out Share Knowledge meeting points as a team

summary.title: Discovering the First Cure
bullets: 5 same-color cards + research station + 1 action = cure
Cured disease: treating removes ALL cubes of that color
Huge efficiency boost — 3 more cures to go

summary.title: Common Mistake: Forgetting Legacy Triggers
bullets: Certain events trigger Legacy deck draws
Assign 1 player as the Legacy deck watcher
Missing triggers causes confusion later in campaign

summary.title: The Race Against Time
bullets: Player deck = game timer (2 cards/turn)
Count remaining cards, divide by 2 = turns left
When time is short, prioritize cures over treating

summary.title: End of Game: Win or Lose
bullets: Apply permanent changes: city damage, scars, Legacy instructions
Lost month = 1 replay attempt, then move on
Record results for campaign tracking

summary.title: Campaign Tips
bullets: Protect characters — scars accumulate permanently
Protect key cities — damage stickers compound over time
Enjoy the story — let yourself be surprised

summary.title: Congratulations
bullets: Practiced movement, treating, epidemics, sharing, and curing
Key mistakes: not communicating, hoarding, forgetting Share rules, ignoring cubes, splitting up
Ready for the real campaign

summary.title: Before You Begin the Campaign
bullets: Check out General Tips → strategy basics for treating and curing
Reference the Appendix → quick-reference for actions and terms
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Communication Is Everything
walkthrough.text: Pandemic Legacy is won and lost through communication. Before each player's turn, the whole team should briefly discuss priorities. Which cities are about to outbreak? Who is closest? Who has the most cards of one color? Which cards should be shared and where? Even thirty seconds of planning per turn can prevent wasted actions. Never play in silence. Talk through every decision openly because every action matters in this game.

walkthrough.title: Treat Reactively, Cure Proactively
walkthrough.text: New players tend to spend all their time treating disease cubes and never get around to discovering cures. Here is the key insight: treating keeps you alive, but curing is how you win. Do not treat a city with one or two cubes unless it is in immediate danger of outbreak. Focus treating on three-cube cities only and spend the rest of your actions on movement, card sharing, and positioning for cures. The board will always look messy. That is normal. A messy board with three cures discovered beats a clean board with zero cures.

walkthrough.title: Plan Card Sharing Early
walkthrough.text: Discovering a cure requires five same-colored cards on one player. With cards spread across the team, Share Knowledge is essential. But the rule is strict: both players must be in the city matching the card. Start planning card transfers from the very first turn. Identify which color is most achievable based on current hands. Assign one player as the collector for that color and route cards toward them. It takes two to four turns to execute a single share, so start early.

walkthrough.title: Research Station Strategy
walkthrough.text: Many new teams build research stations only when they need to cure. By then it is often too late. Build your first additional station early, ideally by the second or third turn of the game. Place stations at geographic hubs that connect different regions of the board. Good early stations cover areas far from Atlanta so the team can use Shuttle Flight to cross the map efficiently. Remember, you can only have six stations. Do not waste them on remote cities.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Player Deck Timer
walkthrough.text: The player deck is your game clock. Every turn, two cards leave the deck. Count how many cards are left and divide by two to know how many turns remain. In the early game, focus on building infrastructure. As the deck shrinks below twenty cards, shift to pure cure-hunting mode. When the deck hits ten cards, every action must push toward winning. Do not waste turns treating one-cube cities when you only have five turns left to cure two diseases.

walkthrough.title: Know Your Character's Strengths
walkthrough.text: Each character has a unique ability that should dictate their role on the team. The Medic is the best firefighter because treating is vastly more efficient. The Researcher makes Share Knowledge much easier. The Dispatcher can move other players' pawns. The Scientist only needs four cards for a cure instead of five. Lean into your character's strengths rather than playing every character the same way. Discuss roles openly so the team plays to each character's advantages.

walkthrough.title: Campaign Thinking: Protect What Matters
walkthrough.text: In a legacy campaign, every outbreak leaves a mark. Cities that outbreak receive damage stickers that make future games harder. Characters in outbreak cities risk scars. Think ahead across multiple sessions, not just the current game. Sometimes it is worth losing one game cleanly rather than winning while three cities take damage. Protect heavily connected cities because their outbreaks cascade to more neighbors. And protect your best characters by keeping them out of dangerous areas when possible.

walkthrough.title: Event Cards Are Your Safety Net
walkthrough.text: Event cards are incredibly powerful and can be played at any time without costing an action. Do not hoard them forever waiting for the perfect moment, but also do not waste them early on minor problems. Save event cards for genuine emergencies: preventing a chain outbreak, clearing cubes before a critical infection draw, or enabling a clutch cure discovery on a key turn. Also remember that event cards do not count toward your hand limit of seven, so holding them costs nothing.

30 seconds of planning prevents wasted actions
Never play in silence — every action matters

A messy board with 3 cures > clean board with 0 cures
Spend most actions on movement, sharing, and curing

Assign 1 player as color collector from turn 1
Card transfers take 2-4 turns to coordinate

Place at geographic hubs for Shuttle Flight coverage

Stations enable both cures and fast travel

summary.title: Watch the Player Deck Timer
bullets: Remaining cards / 2 = turns left
Below 20 cards: shift to pure cure-hunting

Don't waste late-game turns treating 1-cube cities

summary.title: Know Your Character's Strengths
bullets: Medic: best firefighter (removes all cubes of cured color)
Researcher: easiest card sharing
Dispatcher: can move other players' pawns
Scientist: only needs 4 cards for a cure

summary.title: Campaign Thinking: Protect What Matters
bullets: Outbreaks leave permanent damage stickers on cities
Characters risk permanent scars in outbreak cities
Protect highly-connected hub cities — outbreaks cascade
Sometimes a clean loss beats a damaging win

summary.title: Event Cards Are Your Safety Net
bullets: Playable any time, no action cost, don't count toward hand limit
Save for emergencies: preventing chain outbreaks, enabling cures
Don't hoard forever, but don't waste on minor problems
Each event card is one-time use — gone when played

walkthrough.title: Reading the Infection Deck
walkthrough.text: One of the strongest skills in Pandemic is tracking which infection cards have been drawn and which are coming back. After an epidemic, the discard pile goes on top of the deck. This means you know exactly which cities can be drawn in the next few turns. Use this information to decide where to treat. If a three-cube city's card is on top of the infection deck, someone must clear it immediately. If a three-cube city's card was just drawn, it is safe for now because it is at the bottom of the deck.

walkthrough.title: Outbreak Prevention Hierarchy
walkthrough.text: Not all three-cube cities are equally dangerous. A three-cube city with five connections is far more dangerous than one with only two. When deciding which hot spots to treat first, count the connections. A central city like Istanbul or Hong Kong can cause chain outbreaks that spread across the board. A peripheral city like Santiago might outbreak but only affect one or two neighbors. Prioritize treating high-connectivity three-cube cities first because their outbreak potential is exponentially worse.

walkthrough.title: Efficient Card Collection
walkthrough.text: The fastest way to collect five same-colored cards is to identify which color has the most cards spread across the team in the first few turns. Count all cards of each color. If blue has seven cards across four players, blue is your first cure target. Assign one player as the blue collector and route all blue cards toward them. The Researcher character is incredible for this because they can share any card, not just the matching city. If the Researcher is collecting, the team saves many actions on positioning.

walkthrough.title: The Shuttle Network
walkthrough.text: Two research stations create one shuttle route. Three stations create three routes. Four stations create six routes. The travel network grows quadratically with each new station. Place your second station in a different continent from Atlanta, ideally somewhere that covers a cluster of same-colored cities. Your third station should complete a triangle that gives you reach to all four regions. With three well-placed stations, any player can reach any part of the board in two actions, saving cards for cures instead of travel.

walkthrough.title: Turn Order Optimization
walkthrough.text: Turn order matters more than most teams realize. If Player 1 clears a hot spot and Player 2 then builds a station, they are working in sequence. But if Player 2 could have built the station first, enabling Player 1 to Shuttle Flight to the hot spot, Player 1 saves two movement actions. Talk through turn order at the start of each round. Sometimes the optimal play is for one player to set up an action that another player executes. Think of turns as a shared resource, not individual puzzles.

walkthrough.title: Calculated Risk: When to Let Cities Burn
walkthrough.text: Advanced teams understand that sometimes it is correct to let a city outbreak. If treating that city costs four actions and there is no chain outbreak risk, those four actions might be better spent progressing toward a cure. Evaluate each outbreak threat by asking: will this cascade? Will it push us close to eight outbreaks? Will it deplete a cube supply? If the answer to all three is no, consider accepting the single outbreak and spending those actions on winning instead. In legacy mode, also weigh the permanent damage. A single outbreak on an already-damaged city might be acceptable. A first outbreak on a pristine hub city is not.

walkthrough.title: Upgrade Strategy for the Campaign
walkthrough.text: Between games, choose character upgrades carefully. Upgrades that reduce action costs are generally the strongest because they compound over every future game. A character who can treat for free while passing through cities or build research stations without discarding cards saves one action per use, which adds up to dozens of actions across the campaign. Avoid spreading upgrades evenly across all characters. Concentrate upgrades on your two most-used characters to create specialists that the team can rely on.

walkthrough.title: Managing the Funding Curve
walkthrough.text: The funding system is a subtle strategic lever. When funding is high after a loss, you get extra event cards that can swing the next game. Some experienced groups argue that losing the first attempt of a month is sometimes acceptable because the higher funding makes the replay significantly easier. However, each replay means one more game of potential board damage and character scars. The optimal approach is to play aggressively for the win on every attempt but accept losses gracefully and use the increased funding to bounce back. Never deliberately lose a game just for funding, but do recognize that the system is there to help you recover.

summary.title: Reading the Infection Deck
bullets: After epidemic: discard pile goes on top of infection deck
You know which cities will be drawn next
3-cube city on top of deck = must treat immediately
3-cube city just drawn = safe (card at bottom)

summary.title: Outbreak Prevention Hierarchy

Istanbul, Hong Kong = dangerous hubs (5+ connections)
Peripheral cities (Santiago, etc.) = lower cascade risk
Treat hub hot spots first, periphery second

Researcher character saves many positioning actions

Saves cards for cures instead of Direct/Charter Flights

Proper sequencing saves 2-3 actions per round

If all answers = no, consider accepting the outbreak

entries.definition: Move your pawn to an adjacent city connected by a red line on the board. Costs one action. The most basic movement option, best for short distances.
Discard a city card from your hand to move your pawn directly to that city regardless of current location. Costs one action plus one card.
Discard the city card matching your current location to move your pawn to any city on the board. Costs one action plus one specific card.
Move your pawn from one city with a research station to any other city with a research station. Costs one action, no cards required.
Discard the city card matching your current city to place a research station there. Maximum six stations on the board at once. Costs one action plus one card.
Remove one disease cube from your current city. If the disease has been cured, remove all cubes of that color instead. Costs one action.
Give or take a city card with another player, but both players must be in the city matching the card. Costs one action. Essential for collecting cure sets.
At a research station, discard five city cards of the same color to cure that disease. Flip the cure marker. Costs one action. This is the primary win condition.
The main deck containing city cards, event cards, and epidemic cards. Draw two cards per turn. Also serves as the game timer: when it runs out, the players lose.
A player card showing a specific city name and its color (blue, yellow, black, or red). Used for movement (Direct Flight, Charter Flight), building stations, sharing knowledge, and discovering cures.

A dangerous card shuffled into the player deck. When drawn, increase the infection rate, infect a new city with three cubes from the bottom of the infection deck, then intensify by shuffling the discard onto the infection deck.
Cards drawn during the infect cities step showing which city receives a disease cube. After epidemics, the discard pile is shuffled back on top of the deck, causing repeat infections.
Special one-time-use cards that provide powerful effects. Playable at any time without costing an action. Do not count toward the seven-card hand limit. Removed from the game after use.
Cards from the Legacy deck that are drawn when specific triggers occur during gameplay. They advance the story, introduce new rules, and instruct players to open sealed components.
Cards that define the win conditions for each month. Mandatory objectives must be completed to win. Optional objectives provide campaign bonuses.

Occurs when a fourth cube of the same color would be placed on a city. Instead of adding the cube, move the outbreak marker up one space and place one cube on each connected city. Can trigger chain reactions.
When an outbreak causes a neighboring city to also exceed three cubes, triggering another outbreak. Each city can only outbreak once per chain. The outbreak marker moves for each city in the chain.
Determines how many infection cards are drawn at the end of each turn. Starts at two and increases each time an epidemic occurs. Tracked on the infection rate track.
Tracks the number of outbreaks that have occurred. Ranges from zero to eight. If the marker reaches eight, the players immediately lose the game.
The first step of an epidemic. Move the infection rate marker one space to the right on the infection rate track, increasing the number of infection cards drawn each turn.
The second step of an epidemic. Draw the bottom card of the infection deck and place three disease cubes on that city. If this causes an outbreak, resolve it normally.
The third step of an epidemic. Shuffle the infection discard pile and place it on top of the remaining infection deck. This ensures previously infected cities will be drawn again soon.

When a disease has been cured by discarding five same-colored cards at a research station. Treating a cured disease removes all cubes of that color from the city instead of just one. Does not remove cubes already on the board.
When a cured disease has zero cubes remaining on the entire board. Flip the cure marker to the sunset side. Infection cards of that color are now ignored. Powerful but not required to win.
Players may hold a maximum of seven cards (excluding event cards). If you exceed seven after drawing, you must immediately discard down to seven. Discarded cards go to the player discard pile.
A structure placed on a city that enables Shuttle Flight and is required to Discover a Cure. Built by discarding the matching city card. Maximum of six on the board at any time.

Small colored cubes placed on cities to represent disease spread. Four colors: blue, yellow, black, red. Each city can hold up to three cubes of one color before an outbreak occurs. Running out of any color's supply is a loss condition.
Vial-shaped markers for each disease color. Start vial-side up. Flipped when the cure is discovered. Flipped to the sunset side when the disease is eradicated.
Card showing a player's role and unique special ability. In the legacy campaign, character cards accumulate upgrade stickers and scar stickers that persist between games.
A special story deck that drives the campaign narrative. Cards are drawn when specific trigger conditions are met during gameplay. Never look through this deck ahead of time.
Sealed envelopes and numbered boxes containing new components, rules, and story elements. Only opened when specifically instructed by the Legacy deck. Never open early.

Permanent stickers applied to cities that have suffered outbreaks. Each sticker makes the city harder to operate in during future games. Accumulated damage can cause a city to fall into unrest or collapse.

Permanent stickers applied to character cards when they are present in a city during an outbreak. Scars impose penalties such as reduced actions or limited abilities. Too many scars can remove a character permanently.
Positive permanent stickers applied to characters between games. Upgrade abilities, improve actions, or grant bonuses. Choose carefully because they are permanent and limited.
A bonus established between two characters who end a game in the same city. Grants a benefit when the two characters interact in future games. Applied as a sticker to both character cards.
A campaign mechanic that adjusts difficulty between games. Winning decreases funding (fewer event cards next game). Losing increases funding (more event cards). Acts as a catch-up mechanism.
Calculated after the final game in December. Based on months won, final funding level, and end-game bonus objectives. The rulebook provides a rating scale for overall team performance.

A character role. When treating disease, the Medic removes all cubes of one color from a city, not just one. Once a disease is cured, the Medic automatically removes cubes of that color simply by entering or being in a city.
A character role. The Researcher can give any city card to another player in the same city, not just the card matching the current city. This makes Share Knowledge significantly easier.
A character role. The Scientist only needs four cards of the same color (instead of five) to Discover a Cure. This saves one card and speeds up cure discovery.
A character role. The Dispatcher can move other players' pawns as if they were their own, and can move any pawn to a city containing another pawn. Excellent for coordinating team positioning.
A character role. The Generalist gets five actions per turn instead of four. The extra action provides flexibility for movement, treating, and other tasks each turn.
A character role. The Operations Expert can build a research station in any city without discarding a card. They can also move from a research station to any city by discarding any city card.
A character role. The Quarantine Specialist prevents disease cubes and outbreaks from being placed in their current city and all cities connected to it. A powerful defensive ability.
Disease region covering North America and Western Europe. Cities include New York, London, Paris, Madrid, and others. Blue cubes represent one of the four diseases.
Disease region covering Central/South America and Africa. Cities include Miami, Mexico City, Lagos, Kinshasa, and others. Yellow cubes represent one of the four diseases.
Disease region covering the Middle East, Central Asia, and South Asia. Cities include Cairo, Istanbul, Delhi, Mumbai, and others. Black cubes represent one of the four diseases.
Disease region covering East Asia, Southeast Asia, and Oceania. Cities include Beijing, Shanghai, Tokyo, Sydney, and others. Red cubes represent one of the four diseases.
Starting city for all players and home of the CDC. Begins with a research station. Located in the blue region. All pawns are placed here at the start of each game.
